{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_8e9d0931529c4bd7bdecffbb","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Thread-Safety Approach for Async FastAPI First-Failure State\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document designs the thread-safety approach for safely reading/writing first-failure state across concurrent requests in the async FastAPI application. It identifies race conditions, evaluates locking mechanisms, and documents performance implications.\n6\t\n7\t## Current Implementation Analysis\n8\t\n9\t### Existing Code (NOT Thread-Safe)\n10\t\n11\t```python\n12\t# src/telegram/fallback.py (current)\n13\tdef __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n14\t    self._has_logged_first_failure = False  # ❌ No lock protection\n15\t    self._failure_count = 0\n16\t\n17\tdef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n18\t    # ❌ Synchronous function - not async\n19\t    # ❌ No lock protection on check-and-set\n20\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n21\t        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n22\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ❌ Race condition here\n23\t```\n24\t\n25\t### Race Conditions Identified\n26\t\n27\t#### Race Condition 1: Concurrent First Failures\n28\t\n29\t**Scenario**: Multiple requests fail simultaneously at application startup\n30\t\n31\t```\n32\tTime  T0: Request A fails → checks _has_logged_first_failure (False)\n33\tTime  T1: Request B fails → checks _has_logged_first_failure (False)\n34\tTime  T2: Request A sets flag to True, logs WARNING\n35\tTime  T3: Request B sets flag to True, logs WARNING  ❌ DUPLICATE WARNING\n36\t```\n37\t\n38\t**Impact**: \n39\t- Multiple WARNING logs for the same \"first\" failure\n40\t- Violates the requirement of exactly one WARNING per startup\n41\t- Log spam when bridge is down\n42\t\n43\t#### Race Condition 2: Read-Write Race on failure_count\n44\t\n45\t**Scenario**: Multiple concurrent failures increment the counter\n46\t\n47\t```\n48\tTime  T0: failure_count = 0\n49\tTime  T1: Request A reads failure_count (0)\n50\tTime  T2: Request B reads failure_count (0)\n51\tTime  T3: Request A increments: failure_count = 1\n52\tTime  T4: Request B increments: failure_count = 1  ❌ Lost update (should be 2)\n53\t```\n54\t\n55\t**Impact**:\n56\t- Inaccurate failure count\n57\t- Misleading diagnostics\n58\t- Wrong metrics for monitoring\n59\t\n60\t#### Race Condition 3: Check-Then-Act on Timestamps\n61\t\n62\t**Scenario**: Multiple failures update timestamps concurrently\n63\t\n64\t```\n65\tTime  T0: Request A checks first_failure_timestamp (None)\n66\tTime  T1: Request B checks first_failure_timestamp (None)\n67\tTime  T2: Request A sets first_failure_timestamp = now\n68\tTime  T3: Request B sets first_failure_timestamp = now  ❌ Overwrite\n69\t```\n70\t\n71\t**Impact**:\n72\t- Lost first-failure timestamp accuracy\n73\t- Difficult to correlate with logs\n74\t- Misleading diagnostics\n75\t\n76\t## Locking Mechanisms Evaluation\n77\t\n78\t### Option 1: asyncio.Lock (RECOMMENDED)\n79\t\n80\t**Approach**:\n81\t```python\n82\timport asyncio\n83\t\n84\tclass TelegramFallback:\n85\t    def __init__(self):\n86\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n87\t        self._failure_count = 0\n88\t        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # ✅ Async-native lock\n89\t\n90\t    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n91\t        async with self._first_failure_lock:  # ✅ Serialize critical section\n92\t            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n93\t                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n94\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n95\t                self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n96\t                self._failure_count = 1\n97\t            else:\n98\t                logger.debug(\"Repeated failure...\")\n99\t                self._failure_count += 1\n100\t            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n101\t```\n102\t\n103\t**Pros**:\n104\t- ✅ Designed for async/await contexts\n105\t- ✅ Non-blocking when lock is free\n106\t- ✅ Minimal overhead (fast acquisition)\n107\t- ✅ Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n108\t- ✅ Native to FastAPI's async model\n109\t- ✅ Works with `async with` for clean syntax\n110\t\n111\t**Cons**:\n112\t- Lock only works within the same event loop (not an issue for single-process FastAPI)\n113\t- Requires all call sites to `await` (good - enforces async discipline)\n114\t\n115\t**Verdict**: ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Perfect fit for async FastAPI\n116\t\n117\t---\n118\t\n119\t### Option 2: threading.Lock (NOT RECOMMENDED)\n120\t\n121\t**Approach**:\n122\t```python\n123\timport threading\n124\t\n125\tclass TelegramFallback:\n126\t    def __init__(self):\n127\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n128\t        self._lock = threading.Lock()  # ❌ Wrong for async\n129\t\n130\t    def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n131\t        with self._lock:  # ❌ Blocking - defeats async benefits\n132\t            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n133\t                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n134\t                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n135\t```\n136\t\n137\t**Pros**:\n138\t- Thread-safe across OS threads\n139\t- Familiar API\n140\t\n141\t**Cons**:\n142\t- ❌ **Blocking**: Lock acquisition blocks the event loop\n143\t- ❌ **Wrong model**: FastAPI runs on one thread with cooperative multitasking\n144\t- ❌ **Performance risk**: Blocking call stalls all requests on that worker\n145\t- ❌ **Unnecessary**: asyncio.Lock is designed for this exact pattern\n146\t\n147\t**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Blocking in async context is anti-pattern\n148\t\n149\t---\n150\t\n151\t### Option 3: Module-Level Lock with Singleton (NOT RECOMMENDED)\n152\t\n153\t**Approach**:\n154\t```python\n155\t# Module-level lock\n156\t_first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n157\t\n158\tasync def _handle_send_failure(fallback_instance, error_context: str = \"\"):\n159\t    async with _first_failure_lock:\n160\t        if not fallback_instance._has_logged_first_failure:\n161\t            # ...\n162\t```\n163\t\n164\t**Pros**:\n165\t- Lock shared globally\n166\t\n167\t**Cons**:\n168\t- ❌ **Unclear ownership**: Lock is separate from the state it protects\n169\t- ❌ **Harder to test**: Global state requires manual reset\n170\t- ❌ **Less encapsulated**: State and lock are in different places\n171\t- ❌ **No benefit**: Instance-level lock is sufficient (singleton pattern)\n172\t\n173\t**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Unnecessary complexity\n174\t\n175\t---\n176\t\n177\t### Option 4: Atomic Operations (NOT POSSIBLE)\n178\t\n179\t**Approach**: Use atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) operations\n180\t\n181\t**Problem**: \n182\t- Python has no built-in atomic operations for objects\n183\t- `asyncio` doesn't provide atomic primitives for custom state\n184\t- Would require external libraries (e.g., `threading.atomic` - not async-aware)\n185\t\n186\t**Verdict**: ❌ **NOT VIABLE** - No async-safe atomic operations in Python\n187\t\n188\t---\n189\t\n190\t### Option 5: Queue-Based Serialization (OVERKILL)\n191\t\n192\t**Approach**: Push all failures to a queue, process sequentially\n193\t\n194\t```python\n195\tclass TelegramFallback:\n196\t    def __init__(self):\n197\t        self._failure_queue = asyncio.Queue()\n198\t        self._processor_task = None\n199\t\n200\t    async def _process_failures(self):\n201\t        while True:\n202\t            failure = await self._failure_queue.get()\n203\t            # Process sequentially...\n204\t```\n205\t\n206\t**Pros**:\n207\t- Guaranteed serialization\n208\t- Decouples failure handling from request path\n209\t\n210\t**Cons**:\n211\t- ❌ **Overkill**: Full queue for a single boolean flag\n212\t- ❌ **Complex**: Requires background task lifecycle management\n213\t- ❌ **Latency**: Queue adds indirection overhead\n214\t- ❌ **Backpressure**: Could fill up if bridge is down for long time\n215\t\n216\t**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong tool for the job\n217\t\n218\t---\n219\t\n220\t## Recommended Design: asyncio.Lock with Instance State\n221\t\n222\t### Architecture\n223\t\n224\t```\n225\t┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n226\t│              TelegramFallback (Singleton)                │\n227\t├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n228\t│                                                           │\n229\t│  Instance Variables (protected by _first_failure_lock)  │\n230\t│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │\n231\t│  │ _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False          │   │\n232\t│  │ _failure_count: int = 0                          │   │\n233\t│  │ _first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]      │   │\n234\t│  │ _last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]       │   │\n235\t│  │ _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock                │   │\n236\t│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\n237\t│                                                           │\n238\t│  Methods                                                  │\n239\t│  ├─► async _handle_send_failure()  [under lock]          │\n240\t│  └─► def get_bridge_status()        [no lock needed]     │\n241\t│                                                           │\n242\t└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n243\t\n244\tSingleton Access: get_telegram_fallback() returns the same instance\n245\t```\n246\t\n247\t### Thread-Safe Implementation Pattern\n248\t\n249\t#### Critical Section (Write Access)\n250\t\n251\t```python\n252\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n253\t    \"\"\"\n254\t    Handle a send failure with thread-safe first-failure detection.\n255\t    \n256\t    All state mutations occur under lock protection to prevent\n257\t    race conditions from concurrent async requests.\n258\t    \"\"\"\n259\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n260\t        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n261\t            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n262\t            logger.warning(\n263\t                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n264\t                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n265\t                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n266\t            )\n267\t            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n268\t            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n269\t            self._failure_count = 1\n270\t        else:\n271\t            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n272\t            logger.debug(\n273\t                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n274\t                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n275\t                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n276\t            )\n277\t            self._failure_count += 1  # ✅ Increment under lock\n278\t        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()  # ✅ Update under lock\n279\t```\n280\t\n281\t#### Non-Critical Section (Read Access)\n282\t\n283\t```python\n284\tdef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n285\t    \"\"\"\n286\t    Get the current bridge status.\n287\t    \n288\t    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n289\t    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n290\t    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n291\t    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n292\t    \"\"\"\n293\t    return {\n294\t        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n295\t        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n296\t        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # ✅ Safe to read (no lock)\n297\t        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,  # ✅ Safe\n298\t        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n299\t            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n300\t        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n301\t            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n302\t    }\n303\t```\n304\t\n305\t### Lock Acquisition Flow (Concurrent Failures)\n306\t\n307\t```\n308\tRequest A fails          Request B fails          Request C fails\n309\t     │                        │                        │\n310\t     ▼                        ▼                        ▼\n311\t Acquire lock? YES      Acquire lock? NO       Acquire lock? NO\n312\t (wait for lock)        (queued)               (queued)\n313\t     │                        │                        │\n314\t     ▼                        │                        │\n315\t Check flag=False          │                        │\n316\t Log WARNING               │                        │\n317\t Set flag=True             │                        │\n318\t Update timestamps         │                        │\n319\t Release lock              │                        │\n320\t     │                        ▼                        │\n321\t     │                   Acquire lock? YES            │\n322\t     │                   (wait for lock)              │\n323\t     │                        │                        │\n324\t     │                        ▼                        │\n325\t     │                   Check flag=True              │\n326\t     │                   Log DEBUG                   │\n327\t     │                   Increment count              │\n328\t     │                   Update timestamp             │\n329\t     │                   Release lock                 │\n330\t     │                        │                        ▼\n331\t     └────────────────────────┴─────────────────── Acquire lock? YES\n332\t                                                          (wait for lock)\n333\t                                                               │\n334\t                                                               ▼\n335\t                                                          Check flag=True\n336\t                                                          Log DEBUG\n337\t                                                          Increment count\n338\t                                                          Update timestamp\n339\t                                                          Release lock\n340\t\n341\tResult: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG logs ✅ Correct\n342\t```\n343\t\n344\t## Performance Implications\n345\t\n346\t### Lock Contention Analysis\n347\t\n348\t#### Scenario 1: Bridge Healthy (No Failures)\n349\t**Lock usage**: None (no failures, no lock acquisition)\n350\t**Performance impact**: Zero ✅\n351\t\n352\t#### Scenario 2: First Failure at Startup\n353\t**Lock usage**: Single acquisition for ~1-2ms (logging + state updates)\n354\t**Performance impact**: Negligible ✅\n355\t- Only one WARNING log ever\n356\t- Lock is held for microseconds\n357\t- No contention (first failure is rare)\n358\t\n359\t#### Scenario 3: Multiple Concurrent Failures (Bridge Down)\n360\t**Lock usage**: Each failing request acquires lock sequentially\n361\t**Performance impact**: Minimal ✅\n362\t- Lock acquisition is fast (~0.1ms when free)\n363\t- Contention only occurs when bridge is down (already degraded state)\n364\t- Each request waits 1-2ms max for lock\n365\t- Total latency increase: 2-5ms per request (acceptable)\n366\t\n367\t#### Scenario 4: High Traffic with Bridge Down\n368\t**Lock usage**: Many requests queuing for lock\n369\t**Performance impact**: Bounded ✅\n370\t- Worst case: N requests queue, each takes 2ms under lock\n371\t- Total queue wait time: N × 2ms\n372\t- Example: 100 concurrent failures = 200ms total wait = 2ms average per request\n373\t- **Why this is acceptable**:\n374\t  - Bridge is already down (latency doesn't matter for failed sends)\n375\t  - Sends are already failing (no \"happy path\" to optimize)\n376\t  - Logging overhead dominates (2ms lock is <10% of total)\n377\t\n378\t### Performance Measurements (Estimated)\n379\t\n380\t| Operation | Time (without lock) | Time (with lock) | Overhead |\n381\t|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|\n382\t| Successful send | ~50ms | ~50ms | 0% (no lock used) |\n383\t| First failure | ~10ms (logging) | ~12ms | +20% (2ms lock) |\n384\t| Subsequent failure | ~5ms (DEBUG log) | ~7ms | +40% (2ms lock) |\n385\t| Status check | <1ms | <1ms | 0% (no lock) |\n386\t\n387\t**Conclusion**: Lock overhead is negligible (<5ms) and only affects the error path (already slow).\n388\t\n389\t### Alternatives Considered for Performance\n390\t\n391\t#### Option: Lock-Free with Compare-And-Swap\n392\t**Rejected**: No async-safe CAS operations in Python\n393\t\n394\t#### Option: Per-Request State\n395\t**Rejected**: Would violate \"one WARNING per startup\" requirement\n396\t\n397\t#### Option: Queue-Based Delegation\n398\t**Rejected**: Overkill for simple boolean flag\n399\t\n400\t## Implementation Checklist\n401\t\n402\tFor the implementation bead (adc-44u or similar):\n403\t\n404\t- [ ] Add `import asyncio` to `src/telegram/fallback.py`\n405\t- [ ] Add `self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()` to `__init__()`\n406\t- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (add `async`)\n407\t- [ ] Wrap all state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n408\t- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n409\t- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures using `asyncio.gather()`\n410\t- [ ] Add test for race condition prevention\n411\t- [ ] Verify only one WARNING is logged for N concurrent failures\n412\t\n413\t## Testing Strategy\n414\t\n415\t### Test 1: Concurrent First Failures\n416\t```python\n417\tasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n418\t    \"\"\"Verify only one WARNING is logged when multiple requests fail concurrently.\"\"\"\n419\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n420\t    \n421\t    # Trigger 10 concurrent failures\n422\t    await asyncio.gather(*[\n423\t        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n424\t        for i in range(10)\n425\t    ])\n426\t    \n427\t    # Verify state\n428\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n429\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 10\n430\t    \n431\t    # Verify logs (count WARNING vs DEBUG)\n432\t    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 9 DEBUG\n433\t```\n434\t\n435\t### Test 2: Sequential Failures After First\n436\t```python\n437\tasync def test_sequential_failures():\n438\t    \"\"\"Verify subsequent failures log at DEBUG level.\"\"\"\n439\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n440\t    \n441\t    # First failure\n442\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error1\")\n443\t    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n444\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 1\n445\t    \n446\t    # Subsequent failures\n447\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error2\")\n448\t    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error3\")\n449\t    \n450\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 3\n451\t    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG\n452\t```\n453\t\n454\t### Test 3: No Lock Contention on Success\n455\t```python\n456\tasync def test_no_lock_on_success():\n457\t    \"\"\"Verify successful sends don't acquire the lock.\"\"\"\n458\t    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n459\t    \n460\t    # Mock successful send\n461\t    with patch.object(fallback, '_send_http_request', return_value=True):\n462\t        await fallback.send_message(\"chat_id\", \"message\")\n463\t    \n464\t    # State unchanged, lock never acquired\n465\t    assert fallback._failure_count == 0\n466\t```\n467\t\n468\t## Summary\n469\t\n470\t### Race Conditions Prevented\n471\t1. ✅ **Duplicate WARNING logs**: Lock serializes check-and-set of `_has_logged_first_failure`\n472\t2. ✅ **Lost updates on counter**: Lock protects `failure_count` increment\n473\t3. ✅ **Timestamp overwrites**: Lock protects timestamp assignments\n474\t\n475\t### Locking Strategy\n476\t- **Mechanism**: `asyncio.Lock` (async-native, non-blocking)\n477\t- **Scope**: Instance-level lock in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n478\t- **Critical section**: All state mutations in `_handle_send_failure()`\n479\t- **Non-critical**: Read-only access (`get_bridge_status()`) requires no lock\n480\t\n481\t### Performance Impact\n482\t- **Happy path** (successful sends): Zero overhead (no lock acquisition)\n483\t- **Error path** (failures): +2-5ms per request (negligible, only affects degraded state)\n484\t- **Worst case** (100 concurrent failures): ~200ms total queue wait = 2ms per request average\n485\t\n486\t### Why This Approach Works\n487\t1. ✅ **Matches FastAPI architecture**: Async-native, non-blocking\n488\t2. ✅ **Simple**: Single lock, clear critical section\n489\t3. ✅ **Performant**: Minimal overhead, only on error path\n490\t4. ✅ **Correct**: Prevents all identified race conditions\n491\t5. ✅ **Maintainable**: Clear pattern, easy to test\n492\t\n493\t### Migration Path\n494\tFrom current (sync, no lock) → new (async, with lock):\n495\t1. Add lock to `__init__()`\n496\t2. Make `_handle_send_failure()` async\n497\t3. Wrap state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n498\t4. Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n499\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"16a4f06c-9124-4965-b67a-6bb8cd860d61","uuid":"0c8bf98d-2b86-401d-b7ff-51b01e19e8d2","timestamp":"2026-07-03T02:12:52.810Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md","content":"# Thread-Safety Approach for Async FastAPI First-Failure State\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document designs the thread-safety approach for safely reading/writing first-failure state across concurrent requests in the async FastAPI application. It identifies race conditions, evaluates locking mechanisms, and documents performance implications.\n\n## Current Implementation Analysis\n\n### Existing Code (NOT Thread-Safe)\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py (current)\ndef __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n    self._has_logged_first_failure = False  # ❌ No lock protection\n    self._failure_count = 0\n\ndef _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    # ❌ Synchronous function - not async\n    # ❌ No lock protection on check-and-set\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n        logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ❌ Race condition here\n```\n\n### Race Conditions Identified\n\n#### Race Condition 1: Concurrent First Failures\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple requests fail simultaneously at application startup\n\n```\nTime  T0: Request A fails → checks _has_logged_first_failure (False)\nTime  T1: Request B fails → checks _has_logged_first_failure (False)\nTime  T2: Request A sets flag to True, logs WARNING\nTime  T3: Request B sets flag to True, logs WARNING  ❌ DUPLICATE WARNING\n```\n\n**Impact**: \n- Multiple WARNING logs for the same \"first\" failure\n- Violates the requirement of exactly one WARNING per startup\n- Log spam when bridge is down\n\n#### Race Condition 2: Read-Write Race on failure_count\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple concurrent failures increment the counter\n\n```\nTime  T0: failure_count = 0\nTime  T1: Request A reads failure_count (0)\nTime  T2: Request B reads failure_count (0)\nTime  T3: Request A increments: failure_count = 1\nTime  T4: Request B increments: failure_count = 1  ❌ Lost update (should be 2)\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Inaccurate failure count\n- Misleading diagnostics\n- Wrong metrics for monitoring\n\n#### Race Condition 3: Check-Then-Act on Timestamps\n\n**Scenario**: Multiple failures update timestamps concurrently\n\n```\nTime  T0: Request A checks first_failure_timestamp (None)\nTime  T1: Request B checks first_failure_timestamp (None)\nTime  T2: Request A sets first_failure_timestamp = now\nTime  T3: Request B sets first_failure_timestamp = now  ❌ Overwrite\n```\n\n**Impact**:\n- Lost first-failure timestamp accuracy\n- Difficult to correlate with logs\n- Misleading diagnostics\n\n## Locking Mechanisms Evaluation\n\n### Option 1: asyncio.Lock (RECOMMENDED)\n\n**Approach**:\n```python\nimport asyncio\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._failure_count = 0\n        self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # ✅ Async-native lock\n\n    async def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n        async with self._first_failure_lock:  # ✅ Serialize critical section\n            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n                self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n                self._failure_count = 1\n            else:\n                logger.debug(\"Repeated failure...\")\n                self._failure_count += 1\n            self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n```\n\n**Pros**:\n- ✅ Designed for async/await contexts\n- ✅ Non-blocking when lock is free\n- ✅ Minimal overhead (fast acquisition)\n- ✅ Serializes the critical section (check + set)\n- ✅ Native to FastAPI's async model\n- ✅ Works with `async with` for clean syntax\n\n**Cons**:\n- Lock only works within the same event loop (not an issue for single-process FastAPI)\n- Requires all call sites to `await` (good - enforces async discipline)\n\n**Verdict**: ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Perfect fit for async FastAPI\n\n---\n\n### Option 2: threading.Lock (NOT RECOMMENDED)\n\n**Approach**:\n```python\nimport threading\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._lock = threading.Lock()  # ❌ Wrong for async\n\n    def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n        with self._lock:  # ❌ Blocking - defeats async benefits\n            if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n                logger.warning(\"First failure...\")\n                self._has_logged_first_failure = True\n```\n\n**Pros**:\n- Thread-safe across OS threads\n- Familiar API\n\n**Cons**:\n- ❌ **Blocking**: Lock acquisition blocks the event loop\n- ❌ **Wrong model**: FastAPI runs on one thread with cooperative multitasking\n- ❌ **Performance risk**: Blocking call stalls all requests on that worker\n- ❌ **Unnecessary**: asyncio.Lock is designed for this exact pattern\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Blocking in async context is anti-pattern\n\n---\n\n### Option 3: Module-Level Lock with Singleton (NOT RECOMMENDED)\n\n**Approach**:\n```python\n# Module-level lock\n_first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n\nasync def _handle_send_failure(fallback_instance, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    async with _first_failure_lock:\n        if not fallback_instance._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # ...\n```\n\n**Pros**:\n- Lock shared globally\n\n**Cons**:\n- ❌ **Unclear ownership**: Lock is separate from the state it protects\n- ❌ **Harder to test**: Global state requires manual reset\n- ❌ **Less encapsulated**: State and lock are in different places\n- ❌ **No benefit**: Instance-level lock is sufficient (singleton pattern)\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Unnecessary complexity\n\n---\n\n### Option 4: Atomic Operations (NOT POSSIBLE)\n\n**Approach**: Use atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) operations\n\n**Problem**: \n- Python has no built-in atomic operations for objects\n- `asyncio` doesn't provide atomic primitives for custom state\n- Would require external libraries (e.g., `threading.atomic` - not async-aware)\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ **NOT VIABLE** - No async-safe atomic operations in Python\n\n---\n\n### Option 5: Queue-Based Serialization (OVERKILL)\n\n**Approach**: Push all failures to a queue, process sequentially\n\n```python\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self):\n        self._failure_queue = asyncio.Queue()\n        self._processor_task = None\n\n    async def _process_failures(self):\n        while True:\n            failure = await self._failure_queue.get()\n            # Process sequentially...\n```\n\n**Pros**:\n- Guaranteed serialization\n- Decouples failure handling from request path\n\n**Cons**:\n- ❌ **Overkill**: Full queue for a single boolean flag\n- ❌ **Complex**: Requires background task lifecycle management\n- ❌ **Latency**: Queue adds indirection overhead\n- ❌ **Backpressure**: Could fill up if bridge is down for long time\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ **REJECTED** - Wrong tool for the job\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Design: asyncio.Lock with Instance State\n\n### Architecture\n\n```\n┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│              TelegramFallback (Singleton)                │\n├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤\n│                                                           │\n│  Instance Variables (protected by _first_failure_lock)  │\n│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │\n│  │ _has_logged_first_failure: bool = False          │   │\n│  │ _failure_count: int = 0                          │   │\n│  │ _first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]      │   │\n│  │ _last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime]       │   │\n│  │ _first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock                │   │\n│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │\n│                                                           │\n│  Methods                                                  │\n│  ├─► async _handle_send_failure()  [under lock]          │\n│  └─► def get_bridge_status()        [no lock needed]     │\n│                                                           │\n└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n\nSingleton Access: get_telegram_fallback() returns the same instance\n```\n\n### Thread-Safe Implementation Pattern\n\n#### Critical Section (Write Access)\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\"):\n    \"\"\"\n    Handle a send failure with thread-safe first-failure detection.\n    \n    All state mutations occur under lock protection to prevent\n    race conditions from concurrent async requests.\n    \"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:  # 🔒 Serialize access\n        if not self._has_logged_first_failure:\n            # First failure - log at WARNING level\n            logger.warning(\n                f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}. \"\n                f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n            )\n            self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # ✅ Set under lock\n            self._first_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()\n            self._failure_count = 1\n        else:\n            # Subsequent failure - log at DEBUG level\n            logger.debug(\n                f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count + 1} \"\n                f\"at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n                f\"Error: {error_context if error_context else 'unknown error'}.\"\n            )\n            self._failure_count += 1  # ✅ Increment under lock\n        self._last_failure_timestamp = datetime.now()  # ✅ Update under lock\n```\n\n#### Non-Critical Section (Read Access)\n\n```python\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"\n    Get the current bridge status.\n    \n    Read operations do NOT require the lock as they:\n    - Read immutable values (bool, int)\n    - Don't require cross-field consistency\n    - Are used for status/reporting, not decision-making\n    \"\"\"\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,  # ✅ Safe to read (no lock)\n        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,  # ✅ Safe\n        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat() \n            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n    }\n```\n\n### Lock Acquisition Flow (Concurrent Failures)\n\n```\nRequest A fails          Request B fails          Request C fails\n     │                        │                        │\n     ▼                        ▼                        ▼\n Acquire lock? YES      Acquire lock? NO       Acquire lock? NO\n (wait for lock)        (queued)               (queued)\n     │                        │                        │\n     ▼                        │                        │\n Check flag=False          │                        │\n Log WARNING               │                        │\n Set flag=True             │                        │\n Update timestamps         │                        │\n Release lock              │                        │\n     │                        ▼                        │\n     │                   Acquire lock? YES            │\n     │                   (wait for lock)              │\n     │                        │                        │\n     │                        ▼                        │\n     │                   Check flag=True              │\n     │                   Log DEBUG                   │\n     │                   Increment count              │\n     │                   Update timestamp             │\n     │                   Release lock                 │\n     │                        │                        ▼\n     └────────────────────────┴─────────────────── Acquire lock? YES\n                                                          (wait for lock)\n                                                               │\n                                                               ▼\n                                                          Check flag=True\n                                                          Log DEBUG\n                                                          Increment count\n                                                          Update timestamp\n                                                          Release lock\n\nResult: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG logs ✅ Correct\n```\n\n## Performance Implications\n\n### Lock Contention Analysis\n\n#### Scenario 1: Bridge Healthy (No Failures)\n**Lock usage**: None (no failures, no lock acquisition)\n**Performance impact**: Zero ✅\n\n#### Scenario 2: First Failure at Startup\n**Lock usage**: Single acquisition for ~1-2ms (logging + state updates)\n**Performance impact**: Negligible ✅\n- Only one WARNING log ever\n- Lock is held for microseconds\n- No contention (first failure is rare)\n\n#### Scenario 3: Multiple Concurrent Failures (Bridge Down)\n**Lock usage**: Each failing request acquires lock sequentially\n**Performance impact**: Minimal ✅\n- Lock acquisition is fast (~0.1ms when free)\n- Contention only occurs when bridge is down (already degraded state)\n- Each request waits 1-2ms max for lock\n- Total latency increase: 2-5ms per request (acceptable)\n\n#### Scenario 4: High Traffic with Bridge Down\n**Lock usage**: Many requests queuing for lock\n**Performance impact**: Bounded ✅\n- Worst case: N requests queue, each takes 2ms under lock\n- Total queue wait time: N × 2ms\n- Example: 100 concurrent failures = 200ms total wait = 2ms average per request\n- **Why this is acceptable**:\n  - Bridge is already down (latency doesn't matter for failed sends)\n  - Sends are already failing (no \"happy path\" to optimize)\n  - Logging overhead dominates (2ms lock is <10% of total)\n\n### Performance Measurements (Estimated)\n\n| Operation | Time (without lock) | Time (with lock) | Overhead |\n|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|----------|\n| Successful send | ~50ms | ~50ms | 0% (no lock used) |\n| First failure | ~10ms (logging) | ~12ms | +20% (2ms lock) |\n| Subsequent failure | ~5ms (DEBUG log) | ~7ms | +40% (2ms lock) |\n| Status check | <1ms | <1ms | 0% (no lock) |\n\n**Conclusion**: Lock overhead is negligible (<5ms) and only affects the error path (already slow).\n\n### Alternatives Considered for Performance\n\n#### Option: Lock-Free with Compare-And-Swap\n**Rejected**: No async-safe CAS operations in Python\n\n#### Option: Per-Request State\n**Rejected**: Would violate \"one WARNING per startup\" requirement\n\n#### Option: Queue-Based Delegation\n**Rejected**: Overkill for simple boolean flag\n\n## Implementation Checklist\n\nFor the implementation bead (adc-44u or similar):\n\n- [ ] Add `import asyncio` to `src/telegram/fallback.py`\n- [ ] Add `self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()` to `__init__()`\n- [ ] Make `_handle_send_failure()` async (add `async`)\n- [ ] Wrap all state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n- [ ] Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n- [ ] Add tests for concurrent failures using `asyncio.gather()`\n- [ ] Add test for race condition prevention\n- [ ] Verify only one WARNING is logged for N concurrent failures\n\n## Testing Strategy\n\n### Test 1: Concurrent First Failures\n```python\nasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify only one WARNING is logged when multiple requests fail concurrently.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Trigger 10 concurrent failures\n    await asyncio.gather(*[\n        fallback._handle_send_failure(f\"error{i}\")\n        for i in range(10)\n    ])\n    \n    # Verify state\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 10\n    \n    # Verify logs (count WARNING vs DEBUG)\n    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 9 DEBUG\n```\n\n### Test 2: Sequential Failures After First\n```python\nasync def test_sequential_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify subsequent failures log at DEBUG level.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # First failure\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error1\")\n    assert fallback._has_logged_first_failure == True\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 1\n    \n    # Subsequent failures\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error2\")\n    await fallback._handle_send_failure(\"error3\")\n    \n    assert fallback._failure_count == 3\n    # Should see: 1 WARNING, 2 DEBUG\n```\n\n### Test 3: No Lock Contention on Success\n```python\nasync def test_no_lock_on_success():\n    \"\"\"Verify successful sends don't acquire the lock.\"\"\"\n    fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Mock successful send\n    with patch.object(fallback, '_send_http_request', return_value=True):\n        await fallback.send_message(\"chat_id\", \"message\")\n    \n    # State unchanged, lock never acquired\n    assert fallback._failure_count == 0\n```\n\n## Summary\n\n### Race Conditions Prevented\n1. ✅ **Duplicate WARNING logs**: Lock serializes check-and-set of `_has_logged_first_failure`\n2. ✅ **Lost updates on counter**: Lock protects `failure_count` increment\n3. ✅ **Timestamp overwrites**: Lock protects timestamp assignments\n\n### Locking Strategy\n- **Mechanism**: `asyncio.Lock` (async-native, non-blocking)\n- **Scope**: Instance-level lock in `TelegramFallback` singleton\n- **Critical section**: All state mutations in `_handle_send_failure()`\n- **Non-critical**: Read-only access (`get_bridge_status()`) requires no lock\n\n### Performance Impact\n- **Happy path** (successful sends): Zero overhead (no lock acquisition)\n- **Error path** (failures): +2-5ms per request (negligible, only affects degraded state)\n- **Worst case** (100 concurrent failures): ~200ms total queue wait = 2ms per request average\n\n### Why This Approach Works\n1. ✅ **Matches FastAPI architecture**: Async-native, non-blocking\n2. ✅ **Simple**: Single lock, clear critical section\n3. ✅ **Performant**: Minimal overhead, only on error path\n4. ✅ **Correct**: Prevents all identified race conditions\n5. ✅ **Maintainable**: Clear pattern, easy to test\n\n### Migration Path\nFrom current (sync, no lock) → new (async, with lock):\n1. Add lock to `__init__()`\n2. Make `_handle_send_failure()` async\n3. Wrap state mutations in `async with self._first_failure_lock:`\n4. Update all call sites to `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`\n","numLines":499,"startLine":1,"totalLines":499}}}

The state prevents duplicate notifications while maintaining diagnostic information.\n6\t\n7\t## State Structure\n8\t\n9\t```python\n10\t@dataclass\n11\tclass FirstFailureState:\n12\t    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n13\t    \n14\t    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n15\t    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n16\t    \"\"\"\n17\t    # Has the first failure been recorded?\n18\t    has_failed: bool = False\n19\t    \n20\t    # When did the first failure occur?\n21\t    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n22\t    \n23\t    # Which channel failed?\n24\t    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n25\t    \n26\t    # What was the error?\n27\t    error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"TelegramError\", \"TimeoutError\"\n28\t    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n29\t    \n30\t    # Total failure count (for diagnostics)\n31\t    total_failures: int = 0\n32\t    \n33\t    # Have we sent the notification about this first failure?\n34\t    notification_sent: bool = False\n35\t    \n36\t    # When did we send the notification?\n37\t    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n38\t```\n39\t\n40\t## Field Descriptions\n41\t\n42\t### Core State\n43\t\n44\t- **`has_failed: bool`** — Sentinel flag indicating whether ANY failure has occurred since startup. This is the primary field checked before triggering \"first failure\" logic.\n45\t\n46\t- **`first_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of the first failure occurrence. Set once on the first failure, never updated. Use UTC timezone-aware datetimes.\n47\t\n48\t- **`channel_id: Optional[str]`** — The Telegram channel ID that failed on the first occurrence. Useful for diagnosing whether a specific channel is problematic.\n49\t\n50\t### Error Information\n51\t\n52\t- **`error_type: Optional[str]`** — Exception class name (e.g., `\"TelegramError\"`, `\"ConnectionError\"`). Helps categorize the failure type.\n53\t\n54\t- **`error_message: Optional[str]`** — The error message or summary. Useful for quick diagnosis without digging through logs.\n55\t\n56\t### Diagnostics\n57\t\n58\t- **`total_failures: int`** — Monotonically increasing counter of ALL failures since startup. Helps assess failure frequency even after the first failure notification.\n59\t\n60\t### Notification Tracking\n61\t\n62\t- **`notification_sent: bool`** — Prevents duplicate notifications. Set to `True` after the first-failure alert is sent.\n63\t\n64\t- **`notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of when the notification was sent. Useful for audit trails and debugging.\n65\t\n66\t## Reset Scenarios\n67\t\n68\t### 1. Application Startup (Automatic)\n69\t\n70\t```python\n71\t# At module load time in src/monitoring/ambient.py\n72\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n73\t```\n74\t\n75\tAll fields default to their initial values (fresh state).\n76\t\n77\t### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action)\n78\t\n79\tIf an admin manually resolves a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking:\n80\t\n81\t```python\n82\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # Fresh instance\n83\t```\n84\t\n85\tOr selectively:\n86\t\n87\t```python\n88\tfirst_failure_state.has_failed = False\n89\tfirst_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\n90\tfirst_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n91\t# Keep total_failures for historical context, or reset to 0\n92\t```\n93\t\n94\t### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification\n95\t\n96\t**IMPORTANT:** The state does NOT automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification. This prevents notification spam if failures continue. The state persists until application restart.\n97\t\n98\t## Thread-Safety Considerations\n99\t\n100\tSince adc is an async FastAPI application, multiple Telegram sends may execute concurrently. The state update MUST be atomic:\n101\t\n102\t```python\n103\t# Pattern: Check-and-set in one operation\n104\tif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n105\t    # This entire block should be protected by a lock if running in threaded context\n106\t    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n107\t    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n108\t    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n109\t    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n110\t    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)\n111\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n112\t    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n113\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n114\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n115\telse:\n116\t    # Subsequent failures — just increment counter\n117\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n118\t    log_failure(error, channel_id)  # No notification\n119\t```\n120\t\n121\t## Implementation Location\n122\t\n123\tThe state instance should live at module level in `src/monitoring/ambient.py`:\n124\t\n125\t```python\n126\t# src/monitoring/ambient.py\n127\tfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\n128\tfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n129\tfrom typing import Optional\n130\t\n131\t@dataclass\n132\tclass FirstFailureState:\n133\t    # ... (as defined above)\n134\t\n135\t# Module-level singleton\n136\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n137\t```\n138\t\n139\tThis ensures:\n140\t1. Single source of truth across all ambient monitoring checks\n141\t2. State persists across requests (in-memory, per-process)\n142\t3. Reset on each uvicorn worker startup (expected behavior)\n143\t\n144\t## Usage Pattern\n145\t\n146\t```python\n147\t# In the Telegram send error handler\n148\tif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n149\t    # First failure! Record and notify\n150\t    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n151\t    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n152\t    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n153\t    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n154\t    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate if needed\n155\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n156\t    \n157\t    await send_first_failure_notification(\n158\t        channel_id=first_failure_state.channel_id,\n159\t        error_type=first_failure_state.error_type,\n160\t        error_message=first_failure_state.error_message,\n161\t        failed_at=first_failure_state.first_failure_at\n162\t    )\n163\t    \n164\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n165\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n166\telse:\n167\t    # Subsequent failure — just count it\n168\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n169\t    logger.warning(\n170\t        f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure (total: {first_failure_state.total_failures}): \"\n171\t        f\"{type(error).__name__}: {error}\"\n172\t    )\n173\t```\n174\t\n175\t## Data Validation\n176\t\n177\t- **`channel_id`** — Should be a valid Telegram channel ID string (e.g., `@channelname` or numeric ID)\n178\t- **`error_type`** — Should be the exception class name, not the full qualified path\n179\t- **`error_message`** — Consider truncating to ~500 chars to prevent excessive storage/logging\n180\t- **Timestamps** — Always use timezone-aware UTC datetimes\n181\t\n182\t## Future Extensions\n183\t\n184\tPotential enhancements not in v1:\n185\t\n186\t- **`last_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Track when the MOST RECENT failure occurred (for health checks)\n187\t- **`consecutive_failures: int`** — Track consecutive vs. total failures (reset on success)\n188\t- **`failure_history: list[FailureRecord]`** — Keep last N failures for pattern analysis\n189\t- **`recovery_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of first successful send after failure\n190\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"16a4f06c-9124-4965-b67a-6bb8cd860d61","uuid":"afaa3ddf-12ce-4a82-96e7-41ed8e877abf","timestamp":"2026-07-03T02:13:00.200Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/docs/first-failure-state-structure.md","content":"# First-Failure State Data Structure\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document defines the data structure for tracking the first Telegram send failure after application startup. The state prevents duplicate notifications while maintaining diagnostic information.\n\n## State Structure\n\n```python\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n    \n    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n    \"\"\"\n    # Has the first failure been recorded?\n    has_failed: bool = False\n    \n    # When did the first failure occur?\n    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \n    # Which channel failed?\n    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n    \n    # What was the error?\n    error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"TelegramError\", \"TimeoutError\"\n    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n    \n    # Total failure count (for diagnostics)\n    total_failures: int = 0\n    \n    # Have we sent the notification about this first failure?\n    notification_sent: bool = False\n    \n    # When did we send the notification?\n    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n```\n\n## Field Descriptions\n\n### Core State\n\n- **`has_failed: bool`** — Sentinel flag indicating whether ANY failure has occurred since startup. This is the primary field checked before triggering \"first failure\" logic.\n\n- **`first_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of the first failure occurrence. Set once on the first failure, never updated. Use UTC timezone-aware datetimes.\n\n- **`channel_id: Optional[str]`** — The Telegram channel ID that failed on the first occurrence. Useful for diagnosing whether a specific channel is problematic.\n\n### Error Information\n\n- **`error_type: Optional[str]`** — Exception class name (e.g., `\"TelegramError\"`, `\"ConnectionError\"`). Helps categorize the failure type.\n\n- **`error_message: Optional[str]`** — The error message or summary. Useful for quick diagnosis without digging through logs.\n\n### Diagnostics\n\n- **`total_failures: int`** — Monotonically increasing counter of ALL failures since startup. Helps assess failure frequency even after the first failure notification.\n\n### Notification Tracking\n\n- **`notification_sent: bool`** — Prevents duplicate notifications. Set to `True` after the first-failure alert is sent.\n\n- **`notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of when the notification was sent. Useful for audit trails and debugging.\n\n## Reset Scenarios\n\n### 1. Application Startup (Automatic)\n\n```python\n# At module load time in src/monitoring/ambient.py\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n```\n\nAll fields default to their initial values (fresh state).\n\n### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action)\n\nIf an admin manually resolves a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking:\n\n```python\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # Fresh instance\n```\n\nOr selectively:\n\n```python\nfirst_failure_state.has_failed = False\nfirst_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\nfirst_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n# Keep total_failures for historical context, or reset to 0\n```\n\n### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification\n\n**IMPORTANT:** The state does NOT automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification. This prevents notification spam if failures continue. The state persists until application restart.\n\n## Thread-Safety Considerations\n\nSince adc is an async FastAPI application, multiple Telegram sends may execute concurrently. The state update MUST be atomic:\n\n```python\n# Pattern: Check-and-set in one operation\nif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    # This entire block should be protected by a lock if running in threaded context\n    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\nelse:\n    # Subsequent failures — just increment counter\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    log_failure(error, channel_id)  # No notification\n```\n\n## Implementation Location\n\nThe state instance should live at module level in `src/monitoring/ambient.py`:\n\n```python\n# src/monitoring/ambient.py\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\nfrom typing import Optional\n\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    # ... (as defined above)\n\n# Module-level singleton\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n```\n\nThis ensures:\n1. Single source of truth across all ambient monitoring checks\n2. State persists across requests (in-memory, per-process)\n3. Reset on each uvicorn worker startup (expected behavior)\n\n## Usage Pattern\n\n```python\n# In the Telegram send error handler\nif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    # First failure! Record and notify\n    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate if needed\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    \n    await send_first_failure_notification(\n        channel_id=first_failure_state.channel_id,\n        error_type=first_failure_state.error_type,\n        error_message=first_failure_state.error_message,\n        failed_at=first_failure_state.first_failure_at\n    )\n    \n    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\nelse:\n    # Subsequent failure — just count it\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    logger.warning(\n        f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure (total: {first_failure_state.total_failures}): \"\n        f\"{type(error).__name__}: {error}\"\n    )\n```\n\n## Data Validation\n\n- **`channel_id`** — Should be a valid Telegram channel ID string (e.g., `@channelname` or numeric ID)\n- **`error_type`** — Should be the exception class name, not the full qualified path\n- **`error_message`** — Consider truncating to ~500 chars to prevent excessive storage/logging\n- **Timestamps** — Always use timezone-aware UTC datetimes\n\n## Future Extensions\n\nPotential enhancements not in v1:\n\n- **`last_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Track when the MOST RECENT failure occurred (for health checks)\n- **`consecutive_failures: int`** — Track consecutive vs. total failures (reset on success)\n- **`failure_history: list[FailureRecord]`** — Keep last N failures for pattern analysis\n- **`recovery_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of first successful send after failure\n","numLines":190,"startLine":1,"totalLines":190}}}
