{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_2c2920441acb4cb4ba4b3af4","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure State Storage Design for Async FastAPI\n2\t\n3\t## Overview\n4\t\n5\tThis document designs **WHERE** the first-failure state lives in the async FastAPI application (aide-de-camp), building on the data structure design from adc-65l3 and the tracking mechanism design from adc-4vhr.\n6\t\n7\t## Storage Options Evaluation\n8\t\n9\t### Option 1: Module-Level Variable ❌\n10\t\n11\t```python\n12\t# src/telegram/fallback.py\n13\t_first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n14\t\n15\tdef get_first_failure_state():\n16\t    return _first_failure_state\n17\t```\n18\t\n19\t**Pros:**\n20\t- Simple, familiar pattern (used in `ambient.py` for `_ambient_monitor`)\n21\t- Easy to access from anywhere in the module\n22\t- Clear singleton semantics\n23\t\n24\t**Cons:**\n25\t- **Thread-safety**: Harder to protect with locks in async context\n26\t- **Testing**: Difficult to reset or mock in tests\n27\t- **Encapsulation**: Global state is harder to reason about\n28\t- **Dependency injection**: Impossible to inject alternative implementations\n29\t\n30\t**Verdict**: ❌ Not recommended for state that requires atomic transitions\n31\t\n32\t---\n33\t\n34\t### Option 2: Class Attribute (Instance Variable) ✅ RECOMMENDED\n35\t\n36\t```python\n37\t# src/telegram/fallback.py\n38\tclass TelegramFallback:\n39\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n40\t        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\")\n41\t        self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n42\t        self._state_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # Protects state transitions\n43\t```\n44\t\n45\t**Pros:**\n46\t- **Encapsulation**: State is scoped to the instance\n47\t- **Thread-safety**: Can protect with instance-level locks\n48\t- **Testing**: Easy to reset or mock via class instantiation\n49\t- **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures single instance\n50\t- **Dependency injection**: Can inject mock tracker in tests\n51\t- **Consistency**: Matches existing `_ambient_monitor` pattern\n52\t\n53\t**Cons:**\n54\t- Slightly more complex than module-level\n55\t- Requires careful lock management\n56\t\n57\t**Verdict**: ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Best balance of simplicity, testability, and thread-safety\n58\t\n59\t---\n60\t\n61\t### Option 3: Request Context (via FastAPI `contextvar`) ❌\n62\t\n63\t```python\n64\tfrom fastapi import Request\n65\t_first_failure_state = ContextVar(\"first_failure_state\")\n66\t\n67\t@app.post(\"/send\")\n68\tasync def send_message(request: Request):\n69\t    state = _first_failure_state.get(None)\n70\t    # ...\n71\t```\n72\t\n73\t**Pros:**\n74\t- Request-scoped state\n75\t- No shared state between requests\n76\t\n77\t**Cons:**\n78\t- **Wrong semantics**: First-failure state is APPLICATION-scoped, not request-scoped\n79\t- **No persistence**: Lost after each request\n80\t- **Cannot track**: Cannot track \"first failure after startup\" across requests\n81\t\n82\t**Verdict**: ❌ Not applicable - wrong scoping model\n83\t\n84\t---\n85\t\n86\t### Option 4: Database (SQLite/PostgreSQL) ❌\n87\t\n88\t```python\n89\tasync def record_failure(error_context: str):\n90\t    async with aiosqlite.connect(\"first_failures.db\") as db:\n91\t        await db.execute(\n92\t            \"INSERT INTO first_failures (startup_time, error_context) VALUES (?, ?)\",\n93\t            (startup_time, error_context)\n94\t        )\n95\t```\n96\t\n97\t**Pros:**\n98\t- Persistent across restarts\n99\t- Queryable for metrics\n100\t- Can build historical analytics\n101\t\n102\t**Cons:**\n103\t- **Overkill**: Too complex for simple in-memory state tracking\n104\t- **I/O overhead**: Database writes on every failure\n105\t- **Contention**: Database locks can become bottleneck\n106\t- **Complexity**: Need schema management, migrations, cleanup\n107\t\n108\t**Verdict**: ❌ Not recommended as primary storage (use as optional persistence layer only)\n109\t\n110\t---\n111\t\n112\t## Recommended Design: Class Attribute with Lock\n113\t\n114\t### Architecture\n115\t\n116\t```\n117\t┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n118\t│                   TelegramFallback                          │\n119\t│                   (singleton via get_*)                     │\n120\t│                                                              │\n121\t│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │\n122\t│  │  _first_failure_state: FirstFailureState            │  │\n123\t│  │  - has_failed: bool = False                           │  │\n124\t│  │  - first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None         │  │\n125\t│  │  - channel_id: Optional[str] = None                    │  │\n126\t│  │  - error_type: Optional[str] = None                   │  │\n127\t│  │  - error_message: Optional[str] = None                │  │\n128\t│  │  - total_failures: int = 0                            │  │\n129\t│  │  - notification_sent: bool = False                    │  │\n130\t│  │  - notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None     │  │\n131\t│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │\n132\t│                                                              │\n133\t│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │\n134\t│  │  _state_lock: asyncio.Lock                            │  │\n135\t│  │  (protects all _first_failure_state mutations)        │  │\n136\t│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │\n137\t└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n138\t```\n139\t\n140\t### Implementation Pattern\n141\t\n142\t```python\n143\t# src/telegram/fallback.py\n144\tfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\n145\tfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n146\tfrom typing import Optional\n147\timport asyncio\n148\timport logging\n149\t\n150\tlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\n151\t\n152\t@dataclass\n153\tclass FirstFailureState:\n154\t    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n155\t    \n156\t    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n157\t    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n158\t    \"\"\"\n159\t    has_failed: bool = False\n160\t    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n161\t    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n162\t    error_type: Optional[str] = None\n163\t    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n164\t    total_failures: int = 0\n165\t    notification_sent: bool = False\n166\t    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n167\t\n168\tclass TelegramFallback:\n169\t    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n170\t        import os\n171\t        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n172\t            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\"\n173\t        )\n174\t        # State storage: class attribute (instance variable)\n175\t        self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n176\t        # Thread-safety: asyncio.Lock protects state mutations\n177\t        self._state_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n178\t        # Optional: persistence layer (env-var controlled)\n179\t        self._persistence_enabled = os.getenv(\"ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB\") is not None\n180\t\n181\t    async def _record_first_failure(\n182\t        self,\n183\t        channel_id: str,\n184\t        error: Exception,\n185\t    ) -> FirstFailureState:\n186\t        \"\"\"Record the first failure with thread-safety guarantees.\n187\t        \n188\t        This method is atomic: concurrent calls will serialize and only\n189\t        the first call will record the first-failure state.\n190\t        \"\"\"\n191\t        async with self._state_lock:\n192\t            # Double-check pattern: lock ensures only one thread sees False\n193\t            if not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n194\t                # First failure! Record all details\n195\t                self._first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n196\t                self._first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n197\t                self._first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n198\t                self._first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n199\t                self._first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate\n200\t                self._first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n201\t                \n202\t                # Optional: persist to database if enabled\n203\t                if self._persistence_enabled:\n204\t                    await self._persist_first_failure()\n205\t                \n206\t                logger.warning(\n207\t                    f\"First Telegram send failure detected: \"\n208\t                    f\"{self._first_failure_state.error_type}: \"\n209\t                    f\"{self._first_failure_state.error_message}\"\n210\t                )\n211\t            else:\n212\t                # Subsequent failure - just increment counter\n213\t                self._first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n214\t                logger.debug(\n215\t                    f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure \"\n216\t                    f\"(total: {self._first_failure_state.total_failures})\"\n217\t                )\n218\t        \n219\t        return self._first_failure_state\n220\t\n221\t    async def _persist_first_failure(self) -> None:\n222\t        \"\"\"Persist first-failure event to database (optional).\"\"\"\n223\t        import os\n224\t        db_path = os.getenv(\"ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB\")\n225\t        if not db_path:\n226\t            return\n227\t        \n228\t        import aiosqlite\n229\t        async with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n230\t            await db.execute(\n231\t                \"\"\"INSERT INTO first_failures \n232\t                   (startup_time, first_failure_time, channel_id, error_type, error_message)\n233\t                   VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)\"\"\",\n234\t                (\n235\t                    datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),  # Approximate startup time\n236\t                    self._first_failure_state.first_failure_at.isoformat(),\n237\t                    self._first_failure_state.channel_id,\n238\t                    self._first_failure_state.error_type,\n239\t                    self._first_failure_state.error_message,\n240\t                )\n241\t            )\n242\t            await db.commit()\n243\t\n244\t    def get_first_failure_state(self) -> FirstFailureState:\n245\t        \"\"\"Get current first-failure state (read-only, for testing/monitoring).\"\"\"\n246\t        return self._first_failure_state\n247\t\n248\t# Global singleton instance (matches existing pattern)\n249\t_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n250\t\n251\tdef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n252\t    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n253\t    global _telegram_fallback\n254\t    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n255\t        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n256\t    return _telegram_fallback\n257\t```\n258\t\n259\t---\n260\t\n261\t## Lifecycle Management\n262\t\n263\t### Initialization Timing\n264\t\n265\t**When does the state initialize?**\n266\t\n267\t```python\n268\t# Lazy initialization - on first use\n269\t_telegram_fallback = None  # Module load time\n270\t\n271\tdef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n272\t    global _telegram_fallback\n273\t    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n274\t        # State initializes HERE - on first call after startup\n275\t        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n276\t    return _telegram_fallback\n277\t```\n278\t\n279\t**Timeline:**\n280\t1. Application startup → uvicorn loads `src.telegram.fallback` module\n281\t2. Module globals initialized (`_telegram_fallback = None`)\n282\t3. FastAPI app starts serving requests\n283\t4. First request calls `get_telegram_fallback()`\n284\t5. **State initialized**: `_first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()` with default values\n285\t\n286\t**Why lazy initialization?**\n287\t- Faster startup (no unnecessary I/O or DB connections)\n288\t- Only initialize when actually needed\n289\t- Consistent with existing `get_ambient_monitor()` pattern\n290\t\n291\t**Alternative: Eager initialization**\n292\t```python\n293\t# At module load time\n294\t_telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Initializes immediately\n295\t\n296\tdef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n297\t    return _telegram_fallback\n298\t```\n299\t- Also valid, but slower startup\n300\t- Use if you want to fail-fast on initialization errors\n301\t\n302\t---\n303\t\n304\t### Reset Behavior\n305\t\n306\t#### 1. Application Startup (Automatic Reset) ✅\n307\t\n308\t```python\n309\t# Process restart → fresh state\n310\t$ uvicorn src.main:app --reload\n311\t# New process → _telegram_fallback = None → new FirstFailureState()\n312\t```\n313\t\n314\t**Behavior:**\n315\t- All fields reset to defaults: `has_failed=False`, `total_failures=0`, etc.\n316\t- This is **desired behavior**: first-failure tracking is per-process\n317\t- No manual reset needed after restart\n318\t\n319\t**When this happens:**\n320\t- Server restart (`systemctl restart aide-de-camp`)\n321\t- Deployment rollback\n322\t- Crash and restart\n323\t- Development: code change with `--reload`\n324\t\n325\t---\n326\t\n327\t#### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action) 🛠️\n328\t\n329\t**Scenario**: Admin manually resolved a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking.\n330\t\n331\t**Option A: Application Restart (Simplest)**\n332\t```bash\n333\t# Just restart the app\n334\tsystemctl restart aide-de-camp\n335\t# State resets automatically\n336\t```\n337\t\n338\t**Option B: Runtime Reset (via API endpoint)**\n339\t```python\n340\t# Add admin endpoint to FastAPI app\n341\t@app.post(\"/admin/reset-first-failure-state\")\n342\tasync def reset_first_failure_state():\n343\t    \"\"\"Reset first-failure tracking (admin only).\"\"\"\n344\t    telegram = get_telegram_fallback()\n345\t    async with telegram._state_lock:\n346\t        telegram._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n347\t    return {\"status\": \"reset\", \"message\": \"First-failure state cleared\"}\n348\t```\n349\t\n350\t**Option C: Selective Reset (Partial)**\n351\t```python\n352\t# Reset only specific fields\n353\tasync def reset_first_failure_tracking():\n354\t    telegram = get_telegram_fallback()\n355\t    async with telegram._state_lock:\n356\t        telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed = False\n357\t        telegram._first_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\n358\t        telegram._first_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n359\t        # Keep total_failures for historical context\n360\t```\n361\t\n362\t**Recommendation**: Use application restart for production (simplest, no added complexity). Runtime reset only needed for long-running processes where restart is expensive.\n363\t\n364\t---\n365\t\n366\t#### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification ⚠️\n367\t\n368\t**IMPORTANT**: The state does **NOT** automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification.\n369\t\n370\t```python\n371\t# ❌ WRONG - auto-reset after notification (DO NOT DO THIS)\n372\tif not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n373\t    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n374\t    self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # <-- WRONG!\n375\t\n376\t# ✅ CORRECT - state persists after notification\n377\tif not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n378\t    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n379\t    # State persists - subsequent failures see has_failed=True\n380\t```\n381\t\n382\t**Why no auto-reset?**\n383\t- Prevents notification spam if failures continue\n384\t- Single \"first failure after startup\" event per process lifetime\n385\t- Diagnostic value: state remains queryable for debugging\n386\t\n387\t---\n388\t\n389\t## Persistence Requirements\n390\t\n391\t### Question: Does the state survive restarts?\n392\t\n393\t**Answer: No (by design).**\n394\t\n395\t### Rationale: Why Not Persist?\n396\t\n397\t**1. Per-Process Semantics**\n398\t- \"First failure after startup\" is inherently process-scoped\n399\t- Each restart is a new startup event\n400\t- Persisting would conflate \"first after THIS startup\" with \"first EVER\"\n401\t\n402\t**2. Diagnostic Clarity**\n403\t- If state persisted, couldn't distinguish \"never failed\" from \"failed before last restart\"\n404\t- Current semantics: `has_failed=False` means \"clean since this startup\"\n405\t\n406\t**3. Simplicity**\n407\t- No database schema needed\n408\t- No migration/cleanup complexity\n409\t- Faster startup (no DB reads)\n410\t\n411\t**4. Failure Detection Use Case**\n412\t- Purpose: Alert on NEW failures after restart\n413\t- If failures persist across restarts, that's a separate problem (service outage)\n414\t- Persistent failures should be detected by monitoring (Prometheus, etc.)\n415\t\n416\t---\n417\t\n418\t### Optional Persistence Layer (Enhancement)\n419\t\n420\t**Purpose**: Historical tracking, not runtime state management.\n421\t\n422\t```python\n423\t# Environment variable controls persistence\n424\t$ export ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB=/tmp/first_failures.db\n425\t```\n426\t\n427\t**Schema**:\n428\t```sql\n429\tCREATE TABLE first_failures (\n430\t    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,\n431\t    startup_time TEXT NOT NULL,\n432\t    first_failure_time TEXT NOT NULL,\n433\t    channel_id TEXT,\n434\t    error_type TEXT,\n435\t    error_message TEXT,\n436\t    resolved INTEGER DEFAULT 0\n437\t);\n438\t```\n439\t\n440\t**Usage**:\n441\t```python\n442\t# In _record_first_failure():\n443\tif self._persistence_enabled:\n444\t    await self._persist_first_failure()  # Write to DB\n445\t```\n446\t\n447\t**Queries**:\n448\t```python\n449\t# Get first-failure history for last 7 days\n450\tasync def get_first_failure_history(days: int = 7):\n451\t    async with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n452\t        cursor = await db.execute(\n453\t            \"\"\"SELECT * FROM first_failures \n454\t               WHERE first_failure_time > datetime('now', ?) \n455\t               ORDER BY first_failure_time DESC\"\"\",\n456\t            (f\"-{days} days\",)\n457\t        )\n458\t        return await cursor.fetchall()\n459\t```\n460\t\n461\t**Benefits**:\n462\t- Post-mortem analysis: \"When did the first failure occur?\"\n463\t- Metrics: \"How many startups had first failures?\"\n464\t- Debugging: Correlate first failures with deployments\n465\t\n466\t**Trade-offs**:\n467\t- Optional: only enabled in production via env var\n468\t- Separate from in-memory state (for runtime decisions)\n469\t- Queryable via API endpoint for monitoring\n470\t\n471\t---\n472\t\n473\t## Thread-Safety Strategy\n474\t\n475\t### Why asyncio.Lock?\n476\t\n477\t**Problem**: FastAPI handles concurrent requests asynchronously. Multiple coroutines can call `_record_first_failure()` simultaneously.\n478\t\n479\t**Race Condition Without Lock**:\n480\t```python\n481\t# Thread 1                    # Thread 2\n482\tif not state.has_failed:     if not state.has_failed:\n483\t                              # Both see False!\n484\tstate.has_failed = True      \n485\tlog WARNING()                state.has_failed = True\n486\t                              log WARNING()  # DUPLICATE!\n487\t```\n488\t\n489\t**Solution**: asyncio.Lock ensures atomic check-and-set.\n490\t\n491\t```python\n492\tasync with self._state_lock:\n493\t    if not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n494\t        # Only one thread can execute this block at a time\n495\t        self._first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n496\t        # ... record details ...\n497\t```\n498\t\n499\t**Lock Acquisition Pattern**:\n500\t- Lock protects ONLY state mutations\n501\t- Held for microseconds (negligible overhead)\n502\t- No I/O inside lock (persistence is outside or optional)\n503\t- Compatible with async/await throughout\n504\t\n505\t**Performance Impact**:\n506\t- Lock contention is minimal (failures are rare)\n507\t- Overhead: ~10-100 microseconds per acquisition\n508\t- Compare to HTTP I/O: 10+ seconds (100,000x slower than lock)\n509\t\n510\t---\n511\t\n512\t## Testing Strategy\n513\t\n514\t### Unit Tests (State Transitions)\n515\t\n516\t```python\n517\timport pytest\n518\tfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n519\t\n520\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n521\tasync def test_first_failure_recording():\n522\t    \"\"\"Verify first failure is recorded correctly.\"\"\"\n523\t    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n524\t    \n525\t    # Initially: no failures\n526\t    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is False\n527\t    assert telegram._first_failure_state.total_failures == 0\n528\t    \n529\t    # Record first failure\n530\t    state = await telegram._record_first_failure(\n531\t        channel_id=\"@test\",\n532\t        error=Exception(\"Test error\")\n533\t    )\n534\t    \n535\t    # Verify state updated\n536\t    assert state.has_failed is True\n537\t    assert state.total_failures == 1\n538\t    assert state.error_type == \"Exception\"\n539\t    assert state.error_message == \"Test error\"\n540\t    assert state.first_failure_at is not None\n541\t    assert isinstance(state.first_failure_at, datetime)\n542\t\n543\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n544\tasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n545\t    \"\"\"Verify only one first failure when multiple failures happen concurrently.\"\"\"\n546\t    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n547\t    \n548\t    # Simulate 10 concurrent failures\n549\t    import asyncio\n550\t    tasks = [\n551\t        telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(f\"Error {i}\"))\n552\t        for i in range(10)\n553\t    ]\n554\t    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)\n555\t    \n556\t    # All should return the same first-failure state\n557\t    for result in results:\n558\t        assert result.has_failed is True\n559\t        assert result.total_failures == 10  # All 10 counted\n560\t    \n561\t    # But first_failure_at should be from the FIRST one only\n562\t    first_timestamp = results[0].first_failure_at\n563\t    assert all(r.first_failure_at == first_timestamp for r in results)\n564\t```\n565\t\n566\t### Integration Tests (Logging Behavior)\n567\t\n568\t```python\n569\timport pytest\n570\timport logging\n571\t\n572\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n573\tasync def test_first_failure_logging(caplog):\n574\t    \"\"\"Verify WARNING on first failure, DEBUG on subsequent.\"\"\"\n575\t    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n576\t    \n577\t    # First failure: WARNING\n578\t    with caplog.at_level(logging.WARNING):\n579\t        await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error 1\"))\n580\t    assert \"First Telegram send failure detected\" in caplog.text\n581\t    \n582\t    # Second failure: DEBUG only\n583\t    caplog.clear()\n584\t    with caplog.at_level(logging.DEBUG):\n585\t        await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error 2\"))\n586\t    assert \"Subsequent Telegram send failure\" in caplog.text\n587\t    assert \"First Telegram send failure\" not in caplog.text\n588\t```\n589\t\n590\t### Reset Tests\n591\t\n592\t```python\n593\t@pytest.mark.asyncio\n594\tasync def test_manual_reset():\n595\t    \"\"\"Verify manual reset clears state correctly.\"\"\"\n596\t    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n597\t    \n598\t    # Record a failure\n599\t    await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error\"))\n600\t    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is True\n601\t    \n602\t    # Reset state\n603\t    async with telegram._state_lock:\n604\t        telegram._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n605\t    \n606\t    # Verify cleared\n607\t    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is False\n608\t    assert telegram._first_failure_state.total_failures == 0\n609\t```\n610\t\n611\t---\n612\t\n613\t## Summary\n614\t\n615\t### Storage Location: Class Attribute (Instance Variable)\n616\t\n617\t**Location**: `TelegramFallback._first_failure_state`\n618\t\n619\t**Rationale**:\n620\t- ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n621\t- ✅ Easy to test (instantiate, reset, mock)\n622\t- ✅ Singleton pattern via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n623\t- ✅ Consistent with existing patterns (`_ambient_monitor`)\n624\t- ✅ Encapsulated (not global state)\n625\t\n626\t### Initialization: Lazy (on First Use)\n627\t\n628\t**Timing**: When `get_telegram_fallback()` is first called\n629\t\n630\t**Why**:\n631\t- Faster startup\n632\t- Only initialize when needed\n633\t- Fail-fast on initialization errors (when first used)\n634\t\n635\t### Reset Behavior\n636\t\n637\t1. **Automatic reset on startup**: All fields reset to defaults (desired)\n638\t2. **Manual reset**: Via application restart or admin endpoint\n639\t3. **No auto-reset after notification**: State persists to prevent spam\n640\t\n641\t### Persistence: In-Memory (No Restart Survival)\n642\t\n643\t**Rationale**:\n644\t- \"First failure after startup\" is inherently process-scoped\n645\t- Clear semantics: `has_failed=False` = clean since this startup\n646\t- Simplicity: no DB schema, migrations, or cleanup\n647\t- Optional persistence via env var for historical tracking (not runtime state)\n648\t\n649\t### Thread-Safety: asyncio.Lock\n650\t\n651\t**Why**:\n652\t- Atomic check-and-set operations\n653\t- Minimal overhead (microseconds)\n654\t- Compatible with async/await\n655\t- Prevents duplicate first-failure notifications\n656\t\n657\t---\n658\t\n659\t## References\n660\t\n661\t- **Data structure design**: `docs/first-failure-state-structure.md` (adc-65l3)\n662\t- **Tracking mechanism design**: `notes/adc-4vhr.md`\n663\t- **Current implementation**: `src/telegram/fallback.py`\n664\t- **Ambient monitoring pattern**: `src/monitoring/ambient.py` (similar singleton pattern)\n665\t\n666\t---\n667\t\n668\t**Document**: adc-2duz  \n669\t**Status**: ✅ Design Complete  \n670\t**Dependencies**: adc-65l3 (data structure), adc-4vhr (tracking mechanism)  \n671\t**Date**: 2026-07-02\n672\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"16a4f06c-9124-4965-b67a-6bb8cd860d61","uuid":"40125ce8-fdcc-4ec6-b40e-3a6e9feb65e5","timestamp":"2026-07-03T02:13:00.405Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/docs/first-failure-state-storage.md","content":"# First-Failure State Storage Design for Async FastAPI\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document designs **WHERE** the first-failure state lives in the async FastAPI application (aide-de-camp), building on the data structure design from adc-65l3 and the tracking mechanism design from adc-4vhr.\n\n## Storage Options Evaluation\n\n### Option 1: Module-Level Variable ❌\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py\n_first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n\ndef get_first_failure_state():\n    return _first_failure_state\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Simple, familiar pattern (used in `ambient.py` for `_ambient_monitor`)\n- Easy to access from anywhere in the module\n- Clear singleton semantics\n\n**Cons:**\n- **Thread-safety**: Harder to protect with locks in async context\n- **Testing**: Difficult to reset or mock in tests\n- **Encapsulation**: Global state is harder to reason about\n- **Dependency injection**: Impossible to inject alternative implementations\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ Not recommended for state that requires atomic transitions\n\n---\n\n### Option 2: Class Attribute (Instance Variable) ✅ RECOMMENDED\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\")\n        self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n        self._state_lock = asyncio.Lock()  # Protects state transitions\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- **Encapsulation**: State is scoped to the instance\n- **Thread-safety**: Can protect with instance-level locks\n- **Testing**: Easy to reset or mock via class instantiation\n- **Singleton pattern**: `get_telegram_fallback()` ensures single instance\n- **Dependency injection**: Can inject mock tracker in tests\n- **Consistency**: Matches existing `_ambient_monitor` pattern\n\n**Cons:**\n- Slightly more complex than module-level\n- Requires careful lock management\n\n**Verdict**: ✅ **RECOMMENDED** - Best balance of simplicity, testability, and thread-safety\n\n---\n\n### Option 3: Request Context (via FastAPI `contextvar`) ❌\n\n```python\nfrom fastapi import Request\n_first_failure_state = ContextVar(\"first_failure_state\")\n\n@app.post(\"/send\")\nasync def send_message(request: Request):\n    state = _first_failure_state.get(None)\n    # ...\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Request-scoped state\n- No shared state between requests\n\n**Cons:**\n- **Wrong semantics**: First-failure state is APPLICATION-scoped, not request-scoped\n- **No persistence**: Lost after each request\n- **Cannot track**: Cannot track \"first failure after startup\" across requests\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ Not applicable - wrong scoping model\n\n---\n\n### Option 4: Database (SQLite/PostgreSQL) ❌\n\n```python\nasync def record_failure(error_context: str):\n    async with aiosqlite.connect(\"first_failures.db\") as db:\n        await db.execute(\n            \"INSERT INTO first_failures (startup_time, error_context) VALUES (?, ?)\",\n            (startup_time, error_context)\n        )\n```\n\n**Pros:**\n- Persistent across restarts\n- Queryable for metrics\n- Can build historical analytics\n\n**Cons:**\n- **Overkill**: Too complex for simple in-memory state tracking\n- **I/O overhead**: Database writes on every failure\n- **Contention**: Database locks can become bottleneck\n- **Complexity**: Need schema management, migrations, cleanup\n\n**Verdict**: ❌ Not recommended as primary storage (use as optional persistence layer only)\n\n---\n\n## Recommended Design: Class Attribute with Lock\n\n### Architecture\n\n```\n┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                   TelegramFallback                          │\n│                   (singleton via get_*)                     │\n│                                                              │\n│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │\n│  │  _first_failure_state: FirstFailureState            │  │\n│  │  - has_failed: bool = False                           │  │\n│  │  - first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None         │  │\n│  │  - channel_id: Optional[str] = None                    │  │\n│  │  - error_type: Optional[str] = None                   │  │\n│  │  - error_message: Optional[str] = None                │  │\n│  │  - total_failures: int = 0                            │  │\n│  │  - notification_sent: bool = False                    │  │\n│  │  - notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None     │  │\n│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │\n│                                                              │\n│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │\n│  │  _state_lock: asyncio.Lock                            │  │\n│  │  (protects all _first_failure_state mutations)        │  │\n│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │\n└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Implementation Pattern\n\n```python\n# src/telegram/fallback.py\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\nfrom typing import Optional\nimport asyncio\nimport logging\n\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\n\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n    \n    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n    \"\"\"\n    has_failed: bool = False\n    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n    error_type: Optional[str] = None\n    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n    total_failures: int = 0\n    notification_sent: bool = False\n    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n\nclass TelegramFallback:\n    def __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n        import os\n        self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n            \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", \"http://telegram-claude-bridge:8000\"\n        )\n        # State storage: class attribute (instance variable)\n        self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n        # Thread-safety: asyncio.Lock protects state mutations\n        self._state_lock = asyncio.Lock()\n        # Optional: persistence layer (env-var controlled)\n        self._persistence_enabled = os.getenv(\"ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB\") is not None\n\n    async def _record_first_failure(\n        self,\n        channel_id: str,\n        error: Exception,\n    ) -> FirstFailureState:\n        \"\"\"Record the first failure with thread-safety guarantees.\n        \n        This method is atomic: concurrent calls will serialize and only\n        the first call will record the first-failure state.\n        \"\"\"\n        async with self._state_lock:\n            # Double-check pattern: lock ensures only one thread sees False\n            if not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n                # First failure! Record all details\n                self._first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n                self._first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n                self._first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n                self._first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n                self._first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate\n                self._first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n                \n                # Optional: persist to database if enabled\n                if self._persistence_enabled:\n                    await self._persist_first_failure()\n                \n                logger.warning(\n                    f\"First Telegram send failure detected: \"\n                    f\"{self._first_failure_state.error_type}: \"\n                    f\"{self._first_failure_state.error_message}\"\n                )\n            else:\n                # Subsequent failure - just increment counter\n                self._first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n                logger.debug(\n                    f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure \"\n                    f\"(total: {self._first_failure_state.total_failures})\"\n                )\n        \n        return self._first_failure_state\n\n    async def _persist_first_failure(self) -> None:\n        \"\"\"Persist first-failure event to database (optional).\"\"\"\n        import os\n        db_path = os.getenv(\"ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB\")\n        if not db_path:\n            return\n        \n        import aiosqlite\n        async with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n            await db.execute(\n                \"\"\"INSERT INTO first_failures \n                   (startup_time, first_failure_time, channel_id, error_type, error_message)\n                   VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)\"\"\",\n                (\n                    datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),  # Approximate startup time\n                    self._first_failure_state.first_failure_at.isoformat(),\n                    self._first_failure_state.channel_id,\n                    self._first_failure_state.error_type,\n                    self._first_failure_state.error_message,\n                )\n            )\n            await db.commit()\n\n    def get_first_failure_state(self) -> FirstFailureState:\n        \"\"\"Get current first-failure state (read-only, for testing/monitoring).\"\"\"\n        return self._first_failure_state\n\n# Global singleton instance (matches existing pattern)\n_telegram_fallback: Optional[TelegramFallback] = None\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    \"\"\"Get or create the global Telegram fallback instance.\"\"\"\n    global _telegram_fallback\n    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    return _telegram_fallback\n```\n\n---\n\n## Lifecycle Management\n\n### Initialization Timing\n\n**When does the state initialize?**\n\n```python\n# Lazy initialization - on first use\n_telegram_fallback = None  # Module load time\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    global _telegram_fallback\n    if _telegram_fallback is None:\n        # State initializes HERE - on first call after startup\n        _telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()\n    return _telegram_fallback\n```\n\n**Timeline:**\n1. Application startup → uvicorn loads `src.telegram.fallback` module\n2. Module globals initialized (`_telegram_fallback = None`)\n3. FastAPI app starts serving requests\n4. First request calls `get_telegram_fallback()`\n5. **State initialized**: `_first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()` with default values\n\n**Why lazy initialization?**\n- Faster startup (no unnecessary I/O or DB connections)\n- Only initialize when actually needed\n- Consistent with existing `get_ambient_monitor()` pattern\n\n**Alternative: Eager initialization**\n```python\n# At module load time\n_telegram_fallback = TelegramFallback()  # Initializes immediately\n\ndef get_telegram_fallback() -> TelegramFallback:\n    return _telegram_fallback\n```\n- Also valid, but slower startup\n- Use if you want to fail-fast on initialization errors\n\n---\n\n### Reset Behavior\n\n#### 1. Application Startup (Automatic Reset) ✅\n\n```python\n# Process restart → fresh state\n$ uvicorn src.main:app --reload\n# New process → _telegram_fallback = None → new FirstFailureState()\n```\n\n**Behavior:**\n- All fields reset to defaults: `has_failed=False`, `total_failures=0`, etc.\n- This is **desired behavior**: first-failure tracking is per-process\n- No manual reset needed after restart\n\n**When this happens:**\n- Server restart (`systemctl restart aide-de-camp`)\n- Deployment rollback\n- Crash and restart\n- Development: code change with `--reload`\n\n---\n\n#### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action) 🛠️\n\n**Scenario**: Admin manually resolved a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking.\n\n**Option A: Application Restart (Simplest)**\n```bash\n# Just restart the app\nsystemctl restart aide-de-camp\n# State resets automatically\n```\n\n**Option B: Runtime Reset (via API endpoint)**\n```python\n# Add admin endpoint to FastAPI app\n@app.post(\"/admin/reset-first-failure-state\")\nasync def reset_first_failure_state():\n    \"\"\"Reset first-failure tracking (admin only).\"\"\"\n    telegram = get_telegram_fallback()\n    async with telegram._state_lock:\n        telegram._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n    return {\"status\": \"reset\", \"message\": \"First-failure state cleared\"}\n```\n\n**Option C: Selective Reset (Partial)**\n```python\n# Reset only specific fields\nasync def reset_first_failure_tracking():\n    telegram = get_telegram_fallback()\n    async with telegram._state_lock:\n        telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed = False\n        telegram._first_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\n        telegram._first_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n        # Keep total_failures for historical context\n```\n\n**Recommendation**: Use application restart for production (simplest, no added complexity). Runtime reset only needed for long-running processes where restart is expensive.\n\n---\n\n#### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification ⚠️\n\n**IMPORTANT**: The state does **NOT** automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification.\n\n```python\n# ❌ WRONG - auto-reset after notification (DO NOT DO THIS)\nif not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n    self._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # <-- WRONG!\n\n# ✅ CORRECT - state persists after notification\nif not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n    # State persists - subsequent failures see has_failed=True\n```\n\n**Why no auto-reset?**\n- Prevents notification spam if failures continue\n- Single \"first failure after startup\" event per process lifetime\n- Diagnostic value: state remains queryable for debugging\n\n---\n\n## Persistence Requirements\n\n### Question: Does the state survive restarts?\n\n**Answer: No (by design).**\n\n### Rationale: Why Not Persist?\n\n**1. Per-Process Semantics**\n- \"First failure after startup\" is inherently process-scoped\n- Each restart is a new startup event\n- Persisting would conflate \"first after THIS startup\" with \"first EVER\"\n\n**2. Diagnostic Clarity**\n- If state persisted, couldn't distinguish \"never failed\" from \"failed before last restart\"\n- Current semantics: `has_failed=False` means \"clean since this startup\"\n\n**3. Simplicity**\n- No database schema needed\n- No migration/cleanup complexity\n- Faster startup (no DB reads)\n\n**4. Failure Detection Use Case**\n- Purpose: Alert on NEW failures after restart\n- If failures persist across restarts, that's a separate problem (service outage)\n- Persistent failures should be detected by monitoring (Prometheus, etc.)\n\n---\n\n### Optional Persistence Layer (Enhancement)\n\n**Purpose**: Historical tracking, not runtime state management.\n\n```python\n# Environment variable controls persistence\n$ export ADC_FIRST_FAILURE_DB=/tmp/first_failures.db\n```\n\n**Schema**:\n```sql\nCREATE TABLE first_failures (\n    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,\n    startup_time TEXT NOT NULL,\n    first_failure_time TEXT NOT NULL,\n    channel_id TEXT,\n    error_type TEXT,\n    error_message TEXT,\n    resolved INTEGER DEFAULT 0\n);\n```\n\n**Usage**:\n```python\n# In _record_first_failure():\nif self._persistence_enabled:\n    await self._persist_first_failure()  # Write to DB\n```\n\n**Queries**:\n```python\n# Get first-failure history for last 7 days\nasync def get_first_failure_history(days: int = 7):\n    async with aiosqlite.connect(db_path) as db:\n        cursor = await db.execute(\n            \"\"\"SELECT * FROM first_failures \n               WHERE first_failure_time > datetime('now', ?) \n               ORDER BY first_failure_time DESC\"\"\",\n            (f\"-{days} days\",)\n        )\n        return await cursor.fetchall()\n```\n\n**Benefits**:\n- Post-mortem analysis: \"When did the first failure occur?\"\n- Metrics: \"How many startups had first failures?\"\n- Debugging: Correlate first failures with deployments\n\n**Trade-offs**:\n- Optional: only enabled in production via env var\n- Separate from in-memory state (for runtime decisions)\n- Queryable via API endpoint for monitoring\n\n---\n\n## Thread-Safety Strategy\n\n### Why asyncio.Lock?\n\n**Problem**: FastAPI handles concurrent requests asynchronously. Multiple coroutines can call `_record_first_failure()` simultaneously.\n\n**Race Condition Without Lock**:\n```python\n# Thread 1                    # Thread 2\nif not state.has_failed:     if not state.has_failed:\n                              # Both see False!\nstate.has_failed = True      \nlog WARNING()                state.has_failed = True\n                              log WARNING()  # DUPLICATE!\n```\n\n**Solution**: asyncio.Lock ensures atomic check-and-set.\n\n```python\nasync with self._state_lock:\n    if not self._first_failure_state.has_failed:\n        # Only one thread can execute this block at a time\n        self._first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n        # ... record details ...\n```\n\n**Lock Acquisition Pattern**:\n- Lock protects ONLY state mutations\n- Held for microseconds (negligible overhead)\n- No I/O inside lock (persistence is outside or optional)\n- Compatible with async/await throughout\n\n**Performance Impact**:\n- Lock contention is minimal (failures are rare)\n- Overhead: ~10-100 microseconds per acquisition\n- Compare to HTTP I/O: 10+ seconds (100,000x slower than lock)\n\n---\n\n## Testing Strategy\n\n### Unit Tests (State Transitions)\n\n```python\nimport pytest\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_first_failure_recording():\n    \"\"\"Verify first failure is recorded correctly.\"\"\"\n    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Initially: no failures\n    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is False\n    assert telegram._first_failure_state.total_failures == 0\n    \n    # Record first failure\n    state = await telegram._record_first_failure(\n        channel_id=\"@test\",\n        error=Exception(\"Test error\")\n    )\n    \n    # Verify state updated\n    assert state.has_failed is True\n    assert state.total_failures == 1\n    assert state.error_type == \"Exception\"\n    assert state.error_message == \"Test error\"\n    assert state.first_failure_at is not None\n    assert isinstance(state.first_failure_at, datetime)\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_concurrent_first_failures():\n    \"\"\"Verify only one first failure when multiple failures happen concurrently.\"\"\"\n    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Simulate 10 concurrent failures\n    import asyncio\n    tasks = [\n        telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(f\"Error {i}\"))\n        for i in range(10)\n    ]\n    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)\n    \n    # All should return the same first-failure state\n    for result in results:\n        assert result.has_failed is True\n        assert result.total_failures == 10  # All 10 counted\n    \n    # But first_failure_at should be from the FIRST one only\n    first_timestamp = results[0].first_failure_at\n    assert all(r.first_failure_at == first_timestamp for r in results)\n```\n\n### Integration Tests (Logging Behavior)\n\n```python\nimport pytest\nimport logging\n\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_first_failure_logging(caplog):\n    \"\"\"Verify WARNING on first failure, DEBUG on subsequent.\"\"\"\n    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # First failure: WARNING\n    with caplog.at_level(logging.WARNING):\n        await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error 1\"))\n    assert \"First Telegram send failure detected\" in caplog.text\n    \n    # Second failure: DEBUG only\n    caplog.clear()\n    with caplog.at_level(logging.DEBUG):\n        await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error 2\"))\n    assert \"Subsequent Telegram send failure\" in caplog.text\n    assert \"First Telegram send failure\" not in caplog.text\n```\n\n### Reset Tests\n\n```python\n@pytest.mark.asyncio\nasync def test_manual_reset():\n    \"\"\"Verify manual reset clears state correctly.\"\"\"\n    telegram = TelegramFallback()\n    \n    # Record a failure\n    await telegram._record_first_failure(\"@test\", Exception(\"Error\"))\n    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is True\n    \n    # Reset state\n    async with telegram._state_lock:\n        telegram._first_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n    \n    # Verify cleared\n    assert telegram._first_failure_state.has_failed is False\n    assert telegram._first_failure_state.total_failures == 0\n```\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\n### Storage Location: Class Attribute (Instance Variable)\n\n**Location**: `TelegramFallback._first_failure_state`\n\n**Rationale**:\n- ✅ Thread-safe with `asyncio.Lock`\n- ✅ Easy to test (instantiate, reset, mock)\n- ✅ Singleton pattern via `get_telegram_fallback()`\n- ✅ Consistent with existing patterns (`_ambient_monitor`)\n- ✅ Encapsulated (not global state)\n\n### Initialization: Lazy (on First Use)\n\n**Timing**: When `get_telegram_fallback()` is first called\n\n**Why**:\n- Faster startup\n- Only initialize when needed\n- Fail-fast on initialization errors (when first used)\n\n### Reset Behavior\n\n1. **Automatic reset on startup**: All fields reset to defaults (desired)\n2. **Manual reset**: Via application restart or admin endpoint\n3. **No auto-reset after notification**: State persists to prevent spam\n\n### Persistence: In-Memory (No Restart Survival)\n\n**Rationale**:\n- \"First failure after startup\" is inherently process-scoped\n- Clear semantics: `has_failed=False` = clean since this startup\n- Simplicity: no DB schema, migrations, or cleanup\n- Optional persistence via env var for historical tracking (not runtime state)\n\n### Thread-Safety: asyncio.Lock\n\n**Why**:\n- Atomic check-and-set operations\n- Minimal overhead (microseconds)\n- Compatible with async/await\n- Prevents duplicate first-failure notifications\n\n---\n\n## References\n\n- **Data structure design**: `docs/first-failure-state-structure.md` (adc-65l3)\n- **Tracking mechanism design**: `notes/adc-4vhr.md`\n- **Current implementation**: `src/telegram/fallback.py`\n- **Ambient monitoring pattern**: `src/monitoring/ambient.py` (similar singleton pattern)\n\n---\n\n**Document**: adc-2duz  \n**Status**: ✅ Design Complete  \n**Dependencies**: adc-65l3 (data structure), adc-4vhr (tracking mechanism)
